text: This is the case of an eighty-two year old patient affected by HCV liver fibrosis with a diagnosis of a single resectable two centimetre hepatocellular carcinoma of the superior border of segment two. Preoperative workup included liver MRI, thoracic CT scan and blood exams. Findings were as follows. Child PEWS score of A five, MELD score of eight, normal platelet count and AFP within normal values. Gastroscopy was normal. After a multidisciplinary discussion, a liver resection was indicated using a robotic minimally invasive approach.

Four robotic ports are placed horizontally two centimeters above the umbilicus, with a 12mm assistant port inserted below the umbilicus on the left side. The surgeon exposes the hepatic pedicle and performs vascular control, opening the lesser omentum. A tape is passed by the assistant to clamp the hepatic pedicle, which is then exteriorized outside the abdomen on the left.

Intraoperative ultrasonography is performed on the liver, localizing the tumor and showing its proximity to the left suprahepatic vein. The ultrasound scans downward on the left lobe to identify the portal pedicles of segments two and three, revealing no other lesions in the liver. The left portion of the triangular ligament is dissected using a hook and bipolar forceps to expose the caval suprahepatic confluence. The assistant operates through an auxiliary port in the left iliac fossa, while pneumoperitoneum is maintained with an air seal system.

The caval suprahepatic confluence is visible as the surgeon marks the transection line with a hook from the left border of the falciform ligament. Intermittent clamping of the hepatic pedicle is performed, followed by parenchymal transection using cheliclasia with Maryland bipolar forceps. The assistant irrigates the forceps while robotic arm number three progressively opens and exposes the transection line. Kelly closure is used to isolate pedicles of liver segments two and three.

A small portal branch leading to the inferior part of liver segment three is clipped with a hemlock clip and divided using scissors. The Cavitron Ultrasonic Surgical Aspirator (CUSA) is employed via robotic arm number three for precise dissection of portal pedicles. Hemlock clips are placed, and division coagulation is achieved using bipolar forceps at the distal part. The transection progresses cranially and then proximally to expose pedicles of segments two and three. The CUSA is introduced through the auxiliary port, guided by robotic arm number three, for elective dissection of branches heading to the left lobe.

The surgeon ensures haemostasis and biliostasis by placing a hemlock clip. The portal pedicle to segment three is isolated, and the inferior part of the left lobe is opened and dissected. A tape is passed around the pedicle of liver segment three, tightened, and a hemlock clip is placed. Scissors are used for vision. Traction is applied to expose the pedicle of segment two, which is dissected. The origin of the left suprahepatic vein is partially exposed along the transection line.

The surgeon passes a thread around the Glissonian pedicle of segment two, tightens the tape, and places a hemlock clip. The pedicle is divided using scissors, progressively opening the transection line and exposing the lateral border of the left lobe. Dissection continues with a hook in the superior part, further isolating the suprahepatic confluence. The left triangular ligament is opened and divided.

Intraoperative ultrasonography is used to assess the relationship between the lesion and the left suprahepatic vein. Dissection is performed towards the medial part of segment two and along the border with segment four A. A small tear on the common trunk is sutured with a six-zero polypropylene X-shaped stitch. The segmental vein originating from segment two is isolated proximally, and the end of the left suprahepatic vein is visible at the common trunk. The segmental vein from segment two is divided, and a five-zero polypropylene suture is applied to the proximal part.

The surgeon isolates the specimen, focusing on the terminal part of the left suprahepatic vein. A tape is placed around the vein for better exposure, and a vascular clamp is applied proximally. The vein is divided with scissors, freeing the specimen completely, which is then pushed laterally. The stump of the left suprahepatic vein is sutured with a double five-zero polypropylene running suture.

Robotic arm number four exerts traction on the proximal part of the running suture to aid in the exposure of the venous stump during suturing. The vascular clamp is opened at the end of the suture, and the closure of the venous stump is visually controlled. The specimen is placed into a bag and extracted through a phalanx steels incision.
